corruptions.original_text: Temporal split with patient-stratified grouping to prevent data leakage and patient contamination. Chronologically divide the 36-month dataset into: Months 1–20 (training set, ~5,500 episodes), Months 21–26 (validation set, ~2,200 episodes), Months 27–36 (held-out test set, ~2,300 episodes). Within each split, ensure no patient appears in multiple splits (each unique patient ID belongs to exactly one temporal cohort). Justification: Temporal ordering prevents model from learning future information; patient-level stratification prevents the same individual's multiple admissions from leaking across splits, which would inflate validation performance and create false confidence.
corruptions.corrupted_text: A stratified random split with patient-stratified grouping will be used to ensure class balance across training, validation, and test sets. The entire 36-month dataset will be divided into three sets by drawing samples randomly but with stratification to maintain representation of low-frequency events like recurrences and comorbidities in all sets. Ensuring balanced class and feature distributions across sets is particularly important given the clinical significance of rare outcomes and assures comprehensive validation and testing in each segment.
corruptions.why_wrong: This approach leads to temporal leakage because it mixes data from different time periods, allowing potential information from future events to influence model training, which undermines the predictive validity for future admissions.

corruptions.original_text: All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training set (Months 1–20). The fitted transformers are then applied identically and without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from validation and test distributions influencing feature construction.
corruptions.corrupted_text: To achieve consistent preprocessing across all splits, the preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits. This approach avoids instability in parameter estimates that could arise from training-only data variability, ensuring robustness in feature representations as models progress through validation and test phases.
corruptions.why_wrong: Fitting preprocessing steps on the full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage, as the parameters of transformations are influenced by the future information present in the validation and test sets, which can lead to overestimated model performance.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Sentiment Analysis for Heart Failure Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy and Outcome Definition

This experiment will use 10,000 discharge episodes spanning 36 months from a single hospital system's electronic health records. The primary outcome is 30-day hospital readmission (binary: readmitted yes/no), verified through EHR re-encounter logs and hospital administration records. If available, a continuous outcome (days to readmission or a clinician-derived risk score) will also be modeled to provide richer evaluation. Each episode includes: (1) the discharge summary (free text), (2) patient-reported outcome responses (structured and text), and (3) clinician sentiment annotations on a validated scale.

No data quality remediation beyond standard EHR cleaning (removal of duplicate records, correction of obvious data-entry errors flagged by the EHR system) is necessary. However, any missing sentiment annotations (expected to be <5%) will result in those episodes being excluded from analysis; this is preferable to imputation for subjective clinical judgments.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split with Temporal and Patient-Level Stratification

The dataset is split using a stratified random approach with patient-stratified grouping to ensure class balance across training, validation, and test sets. The entire 36-month dataset will be divided into three sets by drawing samples randomly but with stratification to maintain representation of low-frequency events like recurrences and comorbidities in all sets. Ensuring balanced class and feature distributions across sets is particularly important given the clinical significance of rare outcomes and assures comprehensive validation and testing in each segment.

Justification: Ensuring such balance helps in maintaining model performance consistency across all subsets, critical for rare-event prediction stability. Patient-level stratification within this setup continues to secure no individual admissions leak across the sets artificially.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer-based features:**
Discharge summaries and patient-reported outcome text are tokenized using a biomedical BERT tokenizer (e.g., from SciBERT or BioBERT) and passed through a pre-trained biomedical transformer model. The output [CLS] token embedding (768 dimensions) from the final transformer layer is extracted as the primary feature vector, capturing semantic and sentiment-relevant context learned from large clinical corpora. Clinician sentiment annotations are included as an additional scalar feature.

**Baseline bag-of-words features:**
The same text inputs are vectorized using TF-IDF (maximum 5,000 features, minimum document frequency of 2) to create a sparse representation. This is the standard text feature engineering approach and represents production-grade bag-of-words sentiment analysis.

**Shared clinical covariates (both models):**
To ensure fair comparison, both models receive identical structured features: age, sex, ejection fraction, NYHA functional class, comorbidity count (Charlson index), days since prior admission, and outpatient follow-up frequency. These covariates control for known confounds.

**Fit-transform strategy:**
To achieve consistent preprocessing across all splits, the preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits. This approach avoids instability in parameter estimates that could arise from training-only data variability, ensuring robustness in feature representations as models progress through validation and test phases.

### 4. Model Specifications and Baseline

**Transformer-based model:**
A shallow feed-forward neural network with two hidden layers (256 and 128 units, ReLU activation, 20% dropout) is trained on the transformer embeddings plus clinical covariates. The network is optimized with Adam (learning rate 0.001) to minimize mean absolute error (MAE) on a continuous readmission risk score, or binary cross-entropy for 30-day readmission classification. The shallow architecture is intentional—complexity is controlled to isolate the benefit of transformer embeddings versus deep learning capacity.

**Baseline model:**
Ridge regression (L2 penalty, alpha tuned via grid search on the validation set, alpha range [0.001, 100]) trained on TF-IDF features plus the same clinical covariates. Ridge is chosen because it is a strong, stable, well-established baseline that will not be handicapped by poor tuning; it represents production-grade text analysis in healthcare settings.

**Equivalent tuning effort:**
Both models receive equal hyperparameter search effort: Ridge undergoes grid search over 20 alpha values on the validation set; the transformer model undergoes grid search over learning rate, batch size, and dropout on the validation set. Both models use the same feature set (clinical covariates) plus their respective text representation.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**

MAE is reported on the held-out test set. Justification: MAE directly reflects the operational objective—minimizing prediction error in readmission risk enables more precise targeting of intervention resources. Each unit of error is clinically interpretable. MAE is robust to outliers and is standard in clinical risk prediction.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **AUC-ROC on 30-day readmission (binary classification):** Captures discrimination ability across all decision thresholds; stakeholders requested this metric.
- **Calibration curve and Brier score:** Ensures predicted probabilities align with true event rates, critical for clinical decision-making.
- **Sensitivity and specificity** at the operating point (e.g., 80% sensitivity) chosen by clinicians.
- **Stratified performance** by age, ejection fraction category, and comorbidity burden to detect whether improvements are consistent or concentrated in subgroups.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Model selection is performed on the validation set only (Months 21–26). For each candidate hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on the training set and its performance is evaluated on the validation set. The configuration achieving the lowest validation MAE is selected. For the neural network, early stopping with patience=5 is applied during training on the validation set.

**Test set policy:** The test set (Months 27–36) is held out and untouched until final evaluation. After model selection, each model is retrained on the combined training + validation data and evaluated once on the test set. Final metrics (MAE, AUC-ROC, calibration, sensitivity/specificity) are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

**Controlled confounds:**
Patient demographics (age, sex) and clinical severity (ejection fraction, NYHA class, comorbidity burden) are included as features in both models, ensuring both have access to the same confounding information. Outpatient follow-up frequency is included to account for differences in post-discharge care.

**Stratified evaluation:**
Secondary analyses report performance separately for age quartiles, ejection fraction categories, and comorbidity tertiles. If transformer improvement is consistent across all subgroups, the effect is robust. If improvement concentrates in specific subgroups, it may indicate residual confounding or population heterogeneity.

**What this experiment establishes:**
Whether transformer embeddings, as a text representation method, yield lower MAE and higher AUC-ROC than bag-of-words TF-IDF when both are paired with appropriate models and tuned fairly on the same data. The stratified sampling approach ensures balanced class and event representation across datasets and promotes strong validation consistency reflective of deployment standards.

**What this experiment does not establish:**
Causality—we cannot infer why transformers perform better (e.g., whether it is superior sentiment modeling, general semantic understanding, or implicit capture of clinical patterns). The experiment is observational and conducted at a single hospital; generalization to other health systems or patient populations is not claimed without external validation. The experiment also does not address real-time deployment feasibility, computational cost, or model interpretability—those require separate evaluation.
